data.title: Path-aware plan field suggestions
data.body:
## Scope
- Improve `ergo plan` unknown-field suggestions so they are accurate for both top-level and nested task fields.

## Non-goals
- No change to existing error envelope shape (`code`, `message`, `invalid`).
- No behavior changes outside `ergo plan` input parsing.

## Constraint
- Keep strict unknown-field rejection via JSON decoding rules.

data.title: spike: identify a path-aware unknown-field strategy
data.body:
## Goal
- Choose a concrete parser/decoder approach that retains unknown-field JSON path context for `ergo plan` input.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Document the selected strategy and rejected alternative(s) in this task body completion note.
- Strategy explicitly supports distinguishing top-level vs `tasks[]` scope for unknown keys.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo -run 'ParsePlanInput|PlanInput'

data.title: implement scope-aware unknown-field suggestions in plan parser
data.body:
## Goal
- Restore helpful nested suggestions (for example `aftre` -> `after`) while preserving safe top-level suggestions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Top-level typos only suggest top-level fields.
- Task-level typos can suggest task-level fields including `after`.
- Parse error contract remains unchanged for callers.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo -run 'ParsePlanInput|PlanInput'

data.title: add regression tests for scoped unknown-field suggestions
data.body:
## Goal
- Lock in expected top-level and nested suggestion behavior with focused tests.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test covers top-level typo not suggesting nested-only keys.
- Test covers nested typo suggesting `after`.
- Existing `ParsePlanInput` tests continue to pass.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo -run 'ParsePlanInput|PlanInput'

## Goal
- Choose a concrete parser/decoder approach that retains unknown-field JSON path context for `ergo plan` input.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Document the selected strategy and rejected alternative(s) in this task body completion note.
- Strategy explicitly supports distinguishing top-level vs `tasks[]` scope for unknown keys.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo -run 'ParsePlanInput|PlanInput'

## Completion Note
- Selected strategy: keep strict `DisallowUnknownFields` decoding for primary parse failures, then infer unknown-field scope by re-reading the original JSON as `map[string]json.RawMessage` and `tasks[]` task-object maps.
- Why this works: we preserve existing parse contracts while recovering enough context to choose safe suggestion candidates by scope.
- Rejected alternative A: replace decoder path with full custom token-stream parser. Reason: higher complexity and risk for little benefit over current schema.
- Rejected alternative B: loosen global suggestion heuristics. Reason: could introduce false-positive suggestions outside `ergo plan`.

## Goal
- Restore helpful nested suggestions (for example `aftre` -> `after`) while preserving safe top-level suggestions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Top-level typos only suggest top-level fields.
- Task-level typos can suggest task-level fields including `after`.
- Parse error contract remains unchanged for callers.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo -run 'ParsePlanInput|PlanInput'

## Completion Note
- Implemented scope inference for unknown fields in `ParsePlanInput` using raw JSON inspection of top-level keys and `tasks[]` object keys.
- Suggestion candidates are now selected by inferred scope: top-level fields (`title`, `body`, `tasks`) vs task fields (`title`, `body`, `after`).
- Added a narrow plan-specific fallback for single adjacent-character swaps so `aftre` can suggest `after` without relaxing global suggestion rules.
- Preserved parse error envelope shape and strict unknown-field rejection behavior.

## Goal
- Lock in expected top-level and nested suggestion behavior with focused tests.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test covers top-level typo not suggesting nested-only keys.
- Test covers nested typo suggesting `after`.
- Existing `ParsePlanInput` tests continue to pass.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo -run 'ParsePlanInput|PlanInput'

## Completion Note
- Updated nested unknown-field test to assert `aftre` now receives `after` suggestion.
- Kept top-level regression test ensuring top-level typos do not suggest nested-only field names.
- Added focused tests for scope detection and plan-specific suggestion fallback behavior.
- Verified targeted parser/validation test suite passes.

data.title: v0.12: Unify task/epic + simplify CLI surface
spike: audit current Kind usage in codebase
Remove Kind type; introduce isContainer helper
Auto-migrate new_epic events on storage load
Enforce container invariants on mutation
Decide and apply output JSON kind-field disposition
Remove new epic CLI subcommand
Remove plan command; move bulk-create into new task
Simplify list flags (--epics removal/rename)
Relax dep rules to any non-ancestor
Rewrite help.txt for unified model
Rewrite quickstart.txt: simplify input modes, reframe state+claim, drop plan
CHANGELOG, version bump, final test pass, release v0.12

data.body:
## Scope
Drop the `task` vs `epic` kind distinction. Every entity is a task. A task becomes a container the moment it gets a child; without children it is a leaf. One-level nesting only (containers cannot themselves have a parent).

Bundle with this change: simplify the surrounding CLI surface to match.

## What changes
- Data model: drop `Kind`; `isContainer(t)` is derived from has-children.
- Containers cannot hold state/claim/results (enforced).
- Containers complete when all children are done/canceled (unchanged).
- `new epic` subcommand removed.
- `plan` command removed; bulk-create payload (`tasks: [...]`) lives on `new task`.
- Dep rules relax: any two non-ancestor tasks may have an edge.
- `list --epics` flag removed/renamed.
- State+claim coupling reframed as one invariant in docs.
- Quickstart input-mode pedagogy trimmed.

## Decisions (locked)
- Naming: keep `epic` as the containment word in CLI (`--epic <id>` flag, `epic` JSON field). Semantics shift from "epic ID" to "parent task ID." The word "epic" persists colloquially for a container task.
- Compat: hard break at v0.12. No aliases.
- Dep rules: any non-ancestor.
- `plan` command: removed entirely.

## Migration
- On storage load, `new_epic` events are accepted and treated as creating a top-level task that will become a container when children reference it.
- `kind` field in stored events becomes informational; not used for runtime behavior.
- Existing `.ergo/` directories must continue to load without manual intervention.

## Non-goals
- Arbitrary-depth nesting (capped at one level).
- Backward-compat aliases for removed commands/flags.
- UI/output redesign beyond what the model change forces.

## Risks
- Migration correctness on real `.ergo/` graphs with deep epic histories.
- Dep-rule relaxation could allow surprising graphs; needs ancestor-check + cycle detection coverage.
- Help/quickstart rewrite must stay accurate after many code changes — easy place for drift.

## Goal
Produce a written audit of every place the `Kind` distinction influences behavior in the codebase. Output is knowledge, not code changes.

## Why this exists
Downstream tasks (remove `Kind`, enforce container invariants, migrate events) need a complete map of where the epic/task split currently matters. Discovering these one-by-one during implementation invites missed cases.

## Method
- Grep for `Kind`, `kindEpic`, `kindTask`, `kindForTask`, `validateDepKinds`, `EpicAssignEvent`, `new_epic`, `EpicID`, and `is_epic`-style booleans.
- Classify each hit: (a) data-model branching, (b) validation rule, (c) output formatting, (d) test only, (e) doc/help string.
- For each non-trivial branch, note the equivalent post-unification behavior ("derived from has-children" / "forbidden" / "no longer needed").

## Acceptance Criteria
- A short audit note (markdown, in the task body via `set --body-stdin`) listing every file:line where Kind-branching occurs, grouped by category.
- Each entry has a one-line note: "delete," "replace with isContainer," "becomes invariant check," etc.
- Dependent tasks (WAGSO2, MRWX6C, O6M5CB, CGBLMB) can be executed mechanically from this audit.

## Validation Gates
- N/A (spike — produces knowledge). Mark `done` once the audit note is attached to this task body.

## Goal
Eliminate the `Kind` type and its associated constants. Replace every behavioral branch on kind with `isContainer(t *Task) bool`, derived from "has any children pointing at this task as parent."

## Context
- `internal/ergo/model.go` defines `Kind`, `kindAny`, `kindTask`, `kindEpic`, `kindForTask`.
- `kindForTask` currently distinguishes by looking at `task.Children` (per FAVEPP audit; verify exact predicate).
- Internal-only refactor — no event format changes here. Output format changes are CGBLMB.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `Kind` type and constants removed from `model.go`.
- New helper `isContainer(task *Task, graph *Graph) bool` (or equivalent — pick the cleanest signature given the audit) returns true iff at least one task lists this task as its `EpicID`.
- Every call site that previously branched on `Kind` now branches on `isContainer`, or has the branch removed if it is no longer needed.
- `validateDepKinds` is replaced (see NPU3WG for the relaxed rule; coordinate so this task leaves a TODO marker that NPU3WG resolves, or simply removes the function and NPU3WG adds the new check).
- All existing tests still pass except those that explicitly assert on `Kind` strings — those tests get updated to assert on `isContainer` or removed if obsolete.

## Validation Gates
- `go build ./...`
- `go test ./...` passes
- `grep -rn "Kind\\|kindEpic\\|kindTask" internal/ergo/` returns only matches in test fixtures the audit explicitly marked as safe

## Goal
Existing `.ergo/events.jsonl` files containing `new_epic` and `epic` event types must load cleanly under the unified model without manual user intervention.

## Context
- Pre-v0.12 events.jsonl uses `type:"new_epic"` for epic creation and `type:"epic"` for epic-assignment (EpicAssignEvent in commands_work.go:537).
- Post-v0.12, all entities are tasks. The simplest migration: on load, treat `new_epic` as `new_task` with empty `epic_id`. `type:"epic"` (assignment) events keep working as parent-assignment events.
- The on-disk format does not need to be rewritten by this task — `ergo compact` can do that later if desired. This task is read-time compatibility only.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Loading an unmodified pre-v0.12 `events.jsonl` produces an in-memory graph where former epics are tasks with no parent that become containers once their children load.
- `ergo show <former-epic-id>` returns the expected task with children.
- `ergo list` includes former epics correctly.
- A unit test loads a fixture `events.jsonl` containing `new_epic` events (copied from this repo's actual `.ergo/events.jsonl`) and asserts the resulting graph shape.
- Stored `kind` field on legacy events is read but ignored for behavior.

## Validation Gates
- `go test ./internal/ergo/ -run TestMigrate` (or whatever name the new test gets)
- Manual: `ergo --dir <copy of current repo .ergo> list` succeeds and shows expected entities

## Goal
Enforce the rules that make the unified model coherent: containers cannot hold state/claim/results, and one-level nesting is preserved.

## Invariants to enforce
1. A task with at least one child (a container) cannot be mutated with `state`, `claim`, `result_path`, or `result_summary`. Attempts return a clear error.
2. A task may only be assigned as a child to a parent that itself has no parent (one-level nesting cap).
3. A task may not become a parent (i.e., have a child assigned to it via `epic` field) if it currently has `state != todo` or a non-empty `claim`. This prevents a leaf with in-flight work from being silently promoted to a container.
4. When a task becomes a container (gains its first child), any future direct state/claim mutation is rejected per rule 1.
5. When a container loses its last child (all children deleted/pruned), it becomes a leaf again and direct state mutation is permitted.

## Acceptance Criteria
- New rejection paths in `set` and `new task --epic <id>` for the above cases.
- Error messages cite the invariant violated (e.g., "cannot set state on a container task; containers complete when all children complete").
- Unit tests for each invariant: state-on-container rejected; claim-on-container rejected; child-of-child rejected; promotion of doing/claimed leaf rejected.
- Existing tests still pass.

## Validation Gates
- `go test ./...` passes
- New tests in `internal/ergo/state_machine_test.go` or a new file covering each numbered invariant above

## Goal
Decide what the JSON output structure should look like now that `kind` is no longer a primary distinction, then apply the chosen shape everywhere it appears.

## Open sub-decision
Two options for the output JSON of `show`, `claim`, `set`, `list`:
- **A. Drop `kind` entirely.** Consumers detect container-ness via presence of a `children` array or a `child_ids` field. Cleanest. Breaks any consumer reading `kind`.
- **B. Replace `kind` with a boolean `container: true|false`.** Explicit, easy to read, also breaks `kind`-reading consumers.

Recommendation: **B**. Explicitness aids agents who scan output; the cost over A is one field. This task should implement B unless reading the audit reveals a reason to prefer A — note the choice in the completion message.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `kind` field removed from all output paths (`output.go`, `tree_view.go`, anywhere JSON serialization references it).
- New `container: bool` field added on task output (or omitted-when-false to keep output terse — pick one and document).
- `ergo --json show <id>` returns the new shape; snapshot/golden tests updated.
- README/docs example outputs updated to match (deferred to TAVOZJ/TUAG5G — this task only updates code + tests).

## Validation Gates
- `go test ./...` passes
- `ergo --json show <epic-id-from-current-repo>` returns container=true; `ergo --json show <leaf-id>` returns container=false

## Goal
Remove `new epic` as a CLI subcommand. After this change, `new task` is the only creation verb. Containers are created implicitly by creating a task and later assigning children to it via `--epic <id>`.

## Context
- `internal/ergo/commands_create.go` currently routes `new epic`. Find and remove that route.
- CLI parser in `cli.go` likely has a subcommand registration.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `ergo new epic` exits with error: `unknown command "epic" for "ergo new"`.
- `ergo new task --title "Auth"` creates a task with no parent, which can later become a container by creating child tasks with `--epic <its-id>`.
- Help text for `new` lists only `task`.
- Tests covering the old `new epic` path are updated or removed.

## Validation Gates
- `go test ./...` passes
- `ergo new epic` returns non-zero exit and a clear error

## Goal
Remove the `plan` subcommand. Bulk creation (parent + children + dependency edges in one atomic write) is folded into `new task` accepting a `tasks: [...]` payload.

## Context
- `commands_plan.go` and `plan_input.go` hold the current implementation.
- `new task` already accepts JSON; extending it to recognize a `tasks: [...]` field is the cleanest path. The bulk-create code should move/be reused, not duplicated.
- Edge semantics from `plan` (`B.after=["A"]` means B depends on A, matching `sequence`) carry over unchanged.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `ergo plan` exits with error: `unknown command "plan"`.
- `printf %s {"title":"Auth","tasks":[{"title":"Middleware"},{"title":"Login","after":["Middleware"]}]} | ergo --json new task` creates the parent task, child tasks, and dependency edges in one atomic operation.
- The atomic-write guarantee is preserved: a malformed task in the payload aborts the whole creation.
- Existing `commands_plan_test.go` / `plan_input_test.go` are migrated to test the new entry point on `new task`.
- `plans.jsonl` and any plan-specific storage paths handled or removed cleanly.

## Validation Gates
- `go test ./...` passes
- `ergo plan` returns non-zero
- Manual: bulk-create the test payload above; verify with `ergo --json show <returned-parent-id>`

## Goal
Reduce `list` flag sprawl. Drop `--epics` (conflated kind-filtering with the now-derived container concept).

## Proposed surface
- `ergo list` — ready leaf tasks (default; current behavior).
- `ergo list --all` — all non-pruned tasks including containers, blocked, doing, etc.
- `ergo list --epic <id>` — scope to a single parent.
- `ergo list --containers` — list only container tasks (replaces `--epics`).

## Acceptance Criteria
- `--epics` flag removed; `--containers` added with equivalent semantics under the unified model.
- `list` output for containers shows child count (consistent with `show`).
- Tests for the new flag; tests for the removed flag deleted or migrated.

## Validation Gates
- `go test ./...` passes
- `ergo list --containers` lists current repo containers; `ergo list --epics` returns "unknown flag"

## Goal
Replace the current "task↔task or epic↔epic, never cross-kind" dependency rule with: **any two non-ancestor tasks may have an edge**.

## Rule (post-change)
- An edge from A to B is valid iff: A != B; B is not an ancestor of A; A is not an ancestor of B; the resulting graph has no cycles.
- "Ancestor" under one-level nesting means: B.epic == A or A.epic == B.
- Container-to-leaf edges are explicitly supported (a container depending on a leaf blocks all of its children, mirroring today's epic-to-epic behavior).
- Leaf-to-container edges are explicitly supported (a leaf is ready only when all children of the container are done/canceled).

## Acceptance Criteria
- `validateDepKinds` is replaced (or removed and its responsibilities folded into the edge-creation path).
- New ancestor-check function rejects ancestor↔descendant edges with a clear error.
- Cycle detection continues to work across the relaxed edge set.
- New tests cover: leaf→container, container→leaf, leaf-in-A→leaf-in-B, ancestor→descendant (rejected), descendant→ancestor (rejected), self-edge (rejected), cycle (rejected).
- "Ready" computation correctly cascades: a leaf depending on a container is ready only when the container is "done" (all children done/canceled).

## Validation Gates
- `go test ./...` passes
- New tests in `internal/ergo/dep_rules_test.go` cover the cases above
- Manual: `ergo sequence <a-leaf> <a-container>` succeeds; `ergo sequence <child> <parent>` returns ancestor error

## Goal
Rewrite `internal/ergo/help.txt` to reflect the unified model and simplified surface. Must remain a complete cold-start reference for agents.

## Must-change sections
- Top paragraph: replace "Epics contain tasks" framing. New framing: "Tasks form a graph. A task with children is a container (colloquially: an epic). Containers complete when their children do."
- COMMANDS: remove `new epic`, remove `plan`. Keep `new task`, `set`, `show`, `list`, `claim`, `sequence`, etc.
- FLAGS FOR new/set: keep `--epic <id>` but reword description: "assign/reassign to parent task ("" to unassign). One-level nesting only."
- JSON INPUT FIELDS: drop the "Epics only support: title, body" line (no longer a kind distinction). Note instead: "Containers cannot accept state/claim/results — those are managed by their children."
- STATE: reframe the state+claim coupling rules as one invariant: **claim and `doing` move together** — claiming sets `doing`; finishing (done/canceled) drops the claim; `error` retains the claim; `blocked` is orthogonal.
- Dep rules note: "deps allowed between any two non-ancestor tasks; cycles forbidden."
- Bulk-create: a one-line example showing `new task` with a `tasks: [...]` payload (replacing what `plan` used to do).

## Acceptance Criteria
- 100% surface coverage preserved (no removed command/flag goes undocumented; no remaining command/flag is missing).
- No mention of `new epic`, `plan` command, `--epics` flag, or `Kind` distinction.
- Length comparable to current (not bloated; not lossy).
- Examples actually work against the v0.12 build.

## Validation Gates
- `ergo --help` output reviewed manually for accuracy after build.
- Every command shown is runnable; every flag exists.
- `go test ./...` still passes (help.txt is embedded; tests may snapshot it).

## Goal
Rewrite `internal/ergo/quickstart.txt` to match the v0.12 model and to streamline the pedagogy. Three goals beyond accuracy: simplify input-mode framing, reframe state+claim as one invariant, drop the `plan` section.

## Sections to rewrite

### §2 CHOOSE INPUT MODE
Replace the "choose between three modes" framing with a single short paragraph:
  "Flags work for short fields. Pipe JSON for structured input. Use `--body-stdin` when the body is multi-line Markdown."
No bullet list, no comparison table.

### §3 AGENT WORKFLOW (state+claim invariant)
Add a one-line statement of the invariant near the top: "Claim and the `doing` state move together. Claiming sets `doing`; finishing drops the claim; `error` retains it; `blocked` is independent."
Remove or rephrase the older split rules so this one sentence is the canonical statement.

### §4d PLAN A FEATURE
Delete the section entirely.
Replace with a short paragraph under §4 (CREATE WORK): "Bulk-create: `new task` accepts a `tasks: [...]` field to create a parent, children, and dependency edges in one atomic call. Use this when seeding a feature plan."
Show one example with the same shape as the old `plan` example.

### §9 EPIC HIERARCHIES (rename + rewrite)
Rename to "CONTAINERS (formerly: epics)." Reframe: "A task with children is a container. Containers complete when all children are done/canceled. Containers cannot hold state, claim, or results."
Add: "One-level nesting only — a task with a parent cannot itself be a parent."

### REFERENCE: STATE MACHINE
Collapse the existing "invariants" bullets into the one-line invariant from §3.

### REFERENCE: RULES
Update dep rule to: "deps: any two non-ancestor tasks; cycles forbidden."

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every command/flag example in quickstart actually works against v0.12.
- No mention of `new epic`, `plan` command, `--epics`, `Kind`, or the old multi-rule state-claim framing.
- Quickstart length is similar to or shorter than current — simplification, not addition.

## Validation Gates
- `ergo quickstart` output reviewed manually.
- Every command in the doc runs successfully.
- `go test ./...` passes.

## Goal
Cut the v0.12 release: changelog entry, version bump, full test sweep, tag.

## Steps
1. Update `CHANGELOG.md` for v0.12.0 with a "Breaking changes" section. Required entries:
   - `new epic` subcommand removed; use `new task` only.
   - `plan` command removed; bulk-create payload (`tasks: [...]`) moved to `new task`.
   - `Kind` data-model distinction removed; entities are now uniformly `task`. A task with children is a container.
   - One-level nesting cap enforced (a task with a parent cannot itself have children).
   - Container tasks cannot hold state/claim/results (enforced).
   - Dep rules relaxed: any two non-ancestor tasks may have an edge.
   - `list --epics` flag removed; use `list --containers`.
   - JSON output: `kind` field replaced with `container: bool` (or omitted-when-false — match what CGBLMB chose).
   - Migration: existing `.ergo/` directories load without intervention; legacy `new_epic` events are accepted at read time.
2. Bump version in the build (`internal/ergo/version.go` or wherever the version literal lives — find via grep `0.11.2`).
3. Run the full test sweep: `go test ./... -race`.
4. Manual smoke test on the existing repo `.ergo/`: `ergo list`, `ergo show <a former-epic-id>`, `ergo --json show <id>`.
5. Tag: `git tag v0.12.0 && git push --tags` (await user confirmation before pushing — this is a release).

## Acceptance Criteria
- CHANGELOG.md has a complete, accurate v0.12.0 entry.
- Version bumped wherever it appears.
- `go test ./... -race` passes.
- Smoke test on real `.ergo/` succeeds.

## Validation Gates
- `go test ./... -race`
- `ergo --version` reports `0.12.0`
- `ergo list` on the existing repo `.ergo/` shows expected entities

## Scope
Drop the `task` vs `epic` kind distinction. Every entity is a task. A task becomes a container the moment it gets a child; without children it is a leaf. One-level nesting only (containers cannot themselves have a parent).

Bundle with this change: simplify the surrounding CLI surface to match.

## What changes
- Data model: drop `Kind`; `isContainer(t)` is derived from has-children.
- Containers cannot hold state/claim/results (enforced).
- Containers complete when all children are done/canceled (unchanged).
- `new epic` subcommand removed.
- `plan` command removed; bulk-create payload (`tasks: [...]`) lives on `new task`.
- `plans.jsonl` remains the source-of-truth event log even after removing the `plan` command.
- Dep rules relax: any two non-ancestor tasks may have an edge.
- `list --epics` flag removed/renamed.
- State+claim coupling reframed as one invariant in docs.
- Quickstart input-mode pedagogy trimmed.
- Repo docs outside embedded help/quickstart are swept for old surface references before release.

## Decisions (locked)
- Naming: keep `epic` as the containment word in CLI (`--epic <id>` flag, `epic` JSON field). Semantics shift from "epic ID" to "parent task ID." The word `epic` persists colloquially for a container task.

## Non-goals

## Risks

- Dep-rule relaxation could allow surprising graphs; needs ancestor-check + cycle detection coverage.

- Repo docs and skill text can drift from embedded docs unless swept explicitly.

## Validation note
- Validate CLI/doc examples against `go run ./cmd/ergo ...` or a freshly rebuilt binary, not a potentially stale installed binary.

## Goal
Remove the `plan` subcommand. Bulk creation (parent + children + dependency edges in one atomic write) is folded into `new task` accepting a `tasks: [...]` payload.

## Context

## Must-change sections

- JSON INPUT FIELDS: drop the "Epics only support: title, body" line (no longer a kind distinction). Note instead: "Containers cannot accept state, claim, or results - those are managed by their children."
- STATE: reframe the state+claim coupling rules as one invariant: **claim and `doing` move together** - claiming sets `doing`; finishing (`done` or `canceled`) drops the claim; `error` retains the claim; `blocked` is orthogonal.
- Dep rules note: "deps allowed between any two non-ancestor tasks; cycles forbidden."
- Bulk-create: a one-line example showing `new task` with a `tasks: [...]` payload (replacing what `plan` used to do).

## Acceptance Criteria
- 100% surface coverage preserved (no removed command or flag goes undocumented; no remaining command or flag is missing).
- No mention of `new epic`, `plan` command, `--epics` flag, or `Kind` distinction.
- Length comparable to current (not bloated; not lossy).
- Examples actually work against the v0.12 build.

## Validation Gates
- `go run ./cmd/ergo --help` output reviewed manually for accuracy after the build.
- Every command shown is runnable; every flag exists.
- `go test ./...` still passes (help.txt is embedded; tests may snapshot it).

## Goal
Rewrite `internal/ergo/quickstart.txt` to match the v0.12 model and to streamline the pedagogy. Three goals beyond accuracy: simplify input-mode framing, reframe state+claim as one invariant, drop the `plan` section.

## Sections to rewrite

### §2 CHOOSE INPUT MODE
Replace the "choose between three modes" framing with a single short paragraph:
  "Flags work for short fields. Pipe JSON for structured input. Use `--body-stdin` when the body is multi-line Markdown."
No bullet list, no comparison table.

### §3 AGENT WORKFLOW (state+claim invariant)
Add a one-line statement of the invariant near the top: "Claim and the `doing` state move together. Claiming sets `doing`; finishing drops the claim; `error` retains it; `blocked` is independent."
Remove or rephrase the older split rules so this one sentence is the canonical statement.

### §4d PLAN A FEATURE
Delete the section entirely.
Replace with a short paragraph under §4 (CREATE WORK): "Bulk-create: `new task` accepts a `tasks: [...]` field to create a parent, children, and dependency edges in one atomic call. Use this when seeding a feature plan."
Show one example with the same shape as the old `plan` example.

### §9 CONTAINERS (formerly: epics)
Rename and reframe: "A task with children is a container. Containers complete when all children are done or canceled. Containers cannot hold state, claim, or results."
Add: "One-level nesting only - a task with a parent cannot itself be a parent."

### REFERENCE: STATE MACHINE
Collapse the existing "invariants" bullets into the one-line invariant from §3.

### REFERENCE: RULES
Update dep rule to: "deps: any two non-ancestor tasks; cycles forbidden."

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every command or flag example in quickstart actually works against v0.12.
- No mention of `new epic`, `plan` command, `--epics`, `Kind`, or the old multi-rule state-claim framing.
- Quickstart length is similar to or shorter than current - simplification, not addition.

## Validation Gates
- `go run ./cmd/ergo quickstart` output reviewed manually.
- Every command in the doc runs successfully.
- `go test ./...` passes.

## Goal
Cut the v0.12 release: changelog entry, version bump, full test sweep, tag.

## Steps
1. Update `CHANGELOG.md` for v0.12.0 with a "Breaking changes" section. Required entries:
   - `new epic` subcommand removed; use `new task` only.
   - `plan` command removed; bulk-create payload (`tasks: [...]`) moved to `new task`.
   - `Kind` data-model distinction removed; entities are now uniformly `task`. A task with children is a container.
   - One-level nesting cap enforced (a task with a parent cannot itself have children).
   - Container tasks cannot hold state, claim, or results (enforced).
   - Dep rules relaxed: any two non-ancestor tasks may have an edge.
   - `list --epics` flag removed; use `list --containers`.
   - JSON output: `kind` field replaced with `container: bool` (or omitted-when-false - match what CGBLMB chose).
   - Migration: existing `.ergo/` directories load without intervention; legacy `new_epic` events are accepted at read time.
2. Update remaining non-embedded docs (`README.md`, `docs/spec.md`, `skills/ergo-feature-planning/SKILL.md`, and any other surfaced docs) so they match the final v0.12 CLI.
3. Bump version in the build (`internal/ergo/version.go` or wherever the version literal lives - find via grep `0.11.2`).
4. Run the full test sweep: `go test ./... -race`.
5. Manual smoke test on the existing repo `.ergo/`: `go run ./cmd/ergo list`, `go run ./cmd/ergo show <a former-epic-id>`, `go run ./cmd/ergo --json show <id>`.
6. Tag: `git tag v0.12.0 && git push --tags` (await user confirmation before pushing - this is a release).

## Acceptance Criteria
- CHANGELOG.md has a complete, accurate v0.12.0 entry.
- Version bumped wherever it appears.
- Full docs surface is coherent: embedded docs, README, skill text, and spec all match the shipped CLI.
- `go test ./... -race` passes.
- Smoke test on real `.ergo/` succeeds.

## Validation Gates
- `go test ./... -race`
- `go run ./cmd/ergo --version` reports `0.12.0`
- `go run ./cmd/ergo list` on the existing repo `.ergo/` shows expected entities

data.title: v0.12 follow-up: correctness and minimalism
data.body:
## Scope
Address the correctness and minimalism gaps found in the shipped v0.12 implementation without expanding the product surface beyond the intended unified task/container model.

## Why
The current branch is directionally right, but a few behaviors still contradict the vision:
- a leaf can be promoted into a claimed/stateful container
- relaxed non-ancestor deps are accepted but not honored by readiness/blocking logic
- bulk-create still exposes legacy plan/epic vocabulary and silently ignores some user mistakes
- executable fixtures still teach removed commands

## Decisions (locked)
- Bulk-create (`new task` with `tasks:[...]`) should use unified container vocabulary in JSON output, not legacy `plan` / `epic` shape.
- Mixed bulk-create payloads should fail closed: reject top-level leaf-only mutation fields (`state`, `claim`, `result_*`, `epic`) when `tasks` is present.
- Historical proposal docs may remain if clearly labeled as proposals; executable artifacts and current-user docs must match the live CLI.

## Non-goals
- No new user-facing workflow beyond fixing the intended v0.12 model.
- No large storage migration unless it is strictly necessary for correctness.

## Success criteria
- Containers never expose leaf-only state/claim/results semantics.
- Non-ancestor dependency rules are reflected in readiness/blocking behavior, not just validation.
- Common operator mistakes around bulk-create fail with clear errors instead of being silently ignored.
- Repo fixtures and smoke coverage match the current CLI.

data.title: Fix container promotion invariants
data.body:
## Goal
- Close the promotion gap where a leaf with state/claim/results can later become a container.
- Make container invariants true across both mutation and promotion paths.

## Context
- Today `set` rejects state/claim on existing containers, but first-child assignment can still promote a dirty leaf into a container.
- This directly contradicts the unified model and produces impossible `show --json` output.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Assigning the first child to a task that has leaf-only state/claim/results is rejected with a clear invariant error, or the code is refactored so promotion cannot preserve those fields. Pick one approach and make it explicit.
- Add coverage for: claimed leaf -> child assignment; non-todo leaf -> child assignment; results-attached leaf -> child assignment.
- `show --json` for a container never returns leaf-only semantics after promotion.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo/...
- go test ./cmd/ergo/...

data.title: Implement true non-ancestor dependency semantics
data.body:
## Goal
- Make relaxed non-ancestor dependency semantics real in readiness/blocking, not just accepted at link time.
- Preserve the v0.12 promise that leaf<->container edges are first-class.

## Context
- Current validation allows leaf->container and container->leaf edges, but readiness still relies on literal task state in ways that preserve old epic-only semantics.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A leaf depending on a container becomes ready when that container is logically complete (all children done/canceled), not when some stored container state changes.
- Children of a container that depends on a leaf/container are blocked until the container's external deps are satisfied.
- Add end-to-end tests for: leaf->container, container->leaf, sibling/cross-container leaf deps, ancestor->descendant rejection, descendant->ancestor rejection, cycle rejection.
- Remove or simplify any readiness logic that still special-cases legacy epic behavior where the unified model can express the rule directly.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo/...
- go test ./cmd/ergo/...

data.title: Trim legacy naming and duplicate container logic
data.body:
## Goal
- Simplify the implementation after the correctness fixes land, keeping only the minimal legacy compatibility needed for stored events.
- Reduce duplicated container/epic/plan concepts in the codebase.

## Context
- The branch still carries `IsEpic`, `isEpic`, `new_epic`, `planOutput`, and related naming in multiple layers. Some of that may be necessary for event compatibility, but some is just internal cruft now.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Audit the surviving legacy compatibility surfaces after the above fixes and remove or consolidate any that are no longer pulling real weight.
- Prefer one source of truth for container behavior; avoid keeping parallel "derived container" and "legacy epic" concepts unless compatibility truly demands it.
- Keep external compatibility only where the event log or stable JSON contract requires it, and document the reason in code comments where it remains.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo/...
- go test ./cmd/ergo/...

## Goal
- Make leaf→container and container→leaf dependency edges behave correctly in readiness/blocking.
- Keep the fix surgical: no readiness rewrite, just teach the existing dep-check to understand containers.

## The Fix
In `isReady` and `isBlocked`, the dep loop checks `other.State != stateDone && other.State != stateCanceled`. When `other` is a container, its `.State` is meaningless — container completeness is derived from children. The fix:

1. In the dep loop, call `isContainer(other, graph)`.
2. If true, substitute `!isEpicComplete(other.ID, graph)` for the state check.
3. Remove `areEpicDepsComplete` — it's now redundant with the generalized dep loop.

That's it. Three lines of logic change plus deletion of a helper.

## Context
- `isReady` at graph.go:583, `isBlocked` at graph.go:611, `areEpicDepsComplete` at graph.go:652.
- The epic-deps check on lines 603-606 and 631-634 becomes unnecessary once the dep loop itself handles containers.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A leaf depending on a container becomes ready only when all container children are done/canceled.
- A leaf depending on a container is blocked while any container child is incomplete.
- Children of a container whose container depends on an external task are blocked until that external dep is satisfied (existing behavior via the dep loop on the container's children — verify, don't break).
- `areEpicDepsComplete` is deleted.
- ancestor→descendant and descendant→ancestor deps remain rejected at link time (no change needed).

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo/...
- go test ./cmd/ergo/...
- New integration tests: leaf→container readiness, container→leaf readiness, cross-container leaf deps.

## Goal
- Remove stale executable artifacts that teach removed commands.
- Add test coverage that catches fixture/CLI drift automatically — not just "at least one smoke test."

## Acceptance Criteria
- `testdata/create-sample-project.sh` (and any variants) updated to use current CLI surface (`new task` with `--body-stdin` / `tasks:[...]`, not `new epic`).
- A `TestFixtureScripts` integration test that:
  1. Builds the ergo binary into a temp dir.
  2. Runs each `testdata/*.sh` script against it with a fresh `--dir`.
  3. Asserts exit code 0.
  4. Asserts the resulting graph has expected task count and at least one container.
  This catches drift the moment a fixture uses removed syntax.
- Sweep `README.md`, `docs/`, `skills/` for removed surface (`new epic`, `ergo plan`, `--epics`). Proposal docs labeled as historical may remain; anything presenting itself as current instructions must be accurate.
- `docs/proposal-ergo-plan.md` gets a one-line header: `> Historical proposal — see help.txt for current behavior.`

## Validation Gates
- go test ./cmd/ergo/... -run TestFixtureScripts
- go test ./...
- grep -rn "new epic\|ergo plan\|--epics" testdata/ README.md docs/ skills/ internal/ergo/help.txt internal/ergo/quickstart.txt → only hits in clearly-labeled historical docs.

## Goal
- Finish the unified `new task` bulk-create path: unified output schema, strict input rejection.

## Output Shape (locked)
Bulk-create returns `createOutput` with `container:true`, plus `children` and `edges`:

```json
{
  "kind": "create",
  "container": true,
  "id": "ABC123",
  "uuid": "...",
  "title": "My feature",
  "state": "todo",
  "created_at": "...",
  "children": [{"id": "DEF456", "title": "subtask 1"}, ...],
  "edges": [{"from_id": "DEF456", "to_id": "GHI789", "type": "depends"}]
}
```

Reuse `createOutput` as the base; extend with `Children` and `Edges` fields. Delete `planOutput`, `planEntityOutput`, `planTaskOutput`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `new task --json` with `tasks:[...]` returns the shape above — no `kind:"plan"`, no `epic` field.
- `tasks:[]` (empty array) → rejected: "tasks array must not be empty".
- Top-level `state`, `claim`, `result_path`, `result_summary`, `epic` when `tasks` is present → rejected with field-specific error listing which fields are invalid in bulk mode.
- Human-mode output prints: `Created container ABC123 "My feature" with N tasks`.
- Existing single-task `new task` behavior unchanged.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo/...
- go test ./cmd/ergo/...
- Integration tests for: valid bulk-create output shape, empty tasks rejection, mixed-mode field rejection, single-task path unaffected.

## Goal
- After correctness fixes land, strip internal legacy naming that no longer earns its keep.
- One source of truth for "is this a container": the derived check. No parallel concepts.

## Specific Targets
1. **`planOutput` / `planEntityOutput` / `planTaskOutput`** — deleted by HP4R5S; verify nothing references them.
2. **`areEpicDepsComplete`** — deleted by 2ZYNNT; verify gone.
3. **`isEpic` helper** — should only survive in event-replay (`replayEvents`) where we read `new_epic` events and set `IsEpic=true`. Everywhere else should use `isContainer(task, graph)`.
4. **`applyContainerDerivation`** — assess whether it's still needed or if `isContainer` at query time is sufficient. If the former, document why; if the latter, delete it.
5. **`Task.IsEpic` field** — keep (needed for event round-trip) but add a comment: "Set during replay of new_epic events. Do not use for behavioral checks — use isContainer()."
6. **`new_epic` event type in `createTaskWithDir`** — bulk-create emits `new_epic` for the container. Consider switching to `new_task` (since the model is unified) unless event-log consumers depend on it. If switching, ensure `replayEvents` still handles both.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `grep -rn "planOutput\|planEntityOutput\|planTaskOutput\|areEpicDepsComplete" internal/` returns zero hits.
- `isEpic()` is called only from `replayEvents` and the one migration path; all behavioral code uses `isContainer`.
- No duplicate "is this a container?" logic remains (either `applyContainerDerivation` or `isContainer` survives, not both doing the same job).
- All tests pass; no behavioral change from this task.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./...
- grep audit commands above return clean.

data.title: v0.13: replace ergo CLI with stdin-body + plan-file interface
spike: pin the exact v0.13 command contract from draft help text
Rewrite command routing and remove obsolete top-level surface
Implement new/set input model: stdin body plus inline JSON arg
Implement plan --file markdown bulk creation
Preserve read/update compatibility for old plan files while writing the new surface
Rewrite help, quickstart, fixtures, and user-facing docs for the new interface
Add integration and compatibility coverage for command semantics and edge cases
Final polish: remove stale vocabulary, run full tests, and prep the hard cutover docs

data.body:
## Goal
- Replace the current v0.12 CLI with the runtime surface sketched in draft-of-new-help-text.md.
- Make the new interface easier for context-free models to use correctly: inspect with list/show, mutate with new/set/claim/sequence, seed plans with plan --file.
- Ship this as a hard cutover: new ergo must read and update old plan files, but the repo docs and surface should move forward without carrying deleted commands as living concepts.

## Context
- The draft changes more than wording: it changes command arguments, stdin semantics, JSON field naming, and the bulk-planning workflow.
- The repo already has local in-progress edits in command wiring, docs, and tests; implementation should work with those changes rather than resetting to v0.12 assumptions.
- The primary manual for agents remains the rendered help and quickstart output.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The runtime help surface matches the draft intent closely enough that a fresh model can inspect, create, update, claim, and plan work without reading raw .ergo storage.
- Old .ergo logs remain readable and mutable by the new binary.
- The repo manuals, fixtures, and user-facing docs teach only the new interface except where compatibility behavior must be documented.
- Test coverage includes command parsing, body/stdin semantics, markdown plan-file parsing, compatibility reads, and failure cases.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./...
- Manual: go run ./cmd/ergo --help
- Manual: go run ./cmd/ergo quickstart

## Goal
- Convert draft-of-new-help-text.md into a precise implementation contract before code work spreads.
- Resolve any ambiguity in the draft into explicit parser and output rules so downstream tasks can be mechanical.

## Context
- The draft introduces positional JSON args for new/set/plan, stdin-as-body semantics, and a narrower field vocabulary.
- We are not preserving deleted surface as current docs; this task should lock what the new surface is, not narrate what it replaced.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Add a completion note to this task body that spells out the exact command forms for new, set, plan, list, and show.
- The note names the accepted JSON argument shapes, stdin behavior, and whether empty stdin/body is allowed for each command.
- The note lists any edge decisions that tests must lock down: malformed inline JSON, missing title, body plus JSON interactions, markdown chunk parsing, and result field behavior.
- Dependent tasks can implement against this note without re-reading the whole draft for interpretation.

## Validation Gates
- Manual review against draft-of-new-help-text.md
- Manual review against go run ./cmd/ergo --help and quickstart to identify every intentional break

## Goal
- Rework the top-level Cobra surface so the runtime commands and usage match the new interface.
- Remove obsolete command and flag plumbing that no longer belongs in the hard-cutover world.

## Context
- The draft reintroduces top-level plan, narrows list flags, and changes how new/set receive structured arguments.
- This is the task that should make the visible command tree simple and unsurprising before input semantics are wired underneath.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The command tree exposed by go run ./cmd/ergo --help matches the new runtime surface: new task, set, show, list, claim, sequence, plan, init, where, prune, compact.
- Removed or renamed command paths fail cleanly with non-zero exits and no misleading fallback help.
- Flag help strings and usage lines reflect the new interface and no longer teach deleted concepts that are not part of the forward surface.
- Command wiring leaves stdin/inline-JSON semantics to the input tasks, but the routes, args, and error boundaries are correct.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./cmd/ergo -run Command
- Manual: go run ./cmd/ergo --help
- Manual: removed-surface smoke checks return non-zero with clear errors

## Goal
- Make new and set follow the new interface: body comes from stdin, and structured fields come from a single inline JSON argument.
- Keep the implementation small and explicit so models do not have to guess among competing input modes.

## Context
- The draft removes the old JSON-stdin object as the primary model and instead uses stdin for Markdown body plus a positional JSON object for metadata.
- This affects parser shape, validation, help text, error messaging, and result attachment semantics.

## Acceptance Criteria
- new task accepts an optional inline JSON object, reads body from stdin, and enforces the required title rule.
- set <id> accepts an optional inline JSON object, reads replacement body from stdin, and supports metadata-only updates when body is omitted if the contract allows it.
- The JSON field vocabulary matches the draft: title, epic, state, claim, result.
- Edge cases are handled cleanly: malformed inline JSON, unknown keys, empty title on new, invalid state/claim combinations, result without path semantics if still required, and stdin/body interactions when no input is piped.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo/... -run New|Set|Input
- go test ./cmd/ergo -run New|Set
- Manual: draft examples for new task and set work against go run ./cmd/ergo

## Goal
- Implement the new plan seeding workflow from a markdown file, as described in the draft.
- Keep it intentionally simple: markdown chunks become child tasks; sequencing remains explicit via sequence.

## Context
- The draft brings back a plan command, but with a different contract than the old JSON-graph plan feature.
- This is a new parser and mutation path, not a restore of the previous v0.11 plan surface.

## Acceptance Criteria
- plan --file <path> accepts an inline JSON object for top-level fields and creates one epic plus children parsed from markdown chunks separated by ---.
- Each markdown chunk requires a # Title and preserves the remaining markdown as the child body.
- The command returns a stable JSON success shape in --json mode with epic id/title and child ids/titles.
- Failure cases are explicit and atomic: missing file, unreadable file, empty chunk, missing title heading, duplicate or malformed chunk structure, malformed inline JSON, and no partial writes on error.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo/... -run Plan
- go test ./cmd/ergo -run Plan
- Manual: the draft plan example works with go run ./cmd/ergo plan --file ...

## Goal
- Ensure the new binary can read and update old plan files without keeping the repo mentally anchored to the old interface.
- Define exactly what "old files still work" means at the storage and mutation layers.

## Context
- The user wants a hard cutover in docs and runtime surface, but not a storage migration that strands existing repos.
- We already have legacy read paths in the repo; this task decides which survive and what new writes should emit.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Existing .ergo logs created by older ergo versions load successfully under the new binary.
- Mutations against those older logs succeed after replay, including new task creation, set, claim, sequence, prune, compact, and plan --file if applicable.
- The new code writes the forward-format events it intends to own, with any legacy event emission limited to compatibility cases that are justified in code comments and tests.
- Compatibility coverage includes at least one fixture copied from an old-style log and one end-to-end mutation against it.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./internal/ergo/... -run Migrate|Legacy|Compact
- go test ./cmd/ergo -run Legacy|Compact|Compatibility
- Manual: run one mutation against a legacy-style .ergo fixture

## Goal
- Rewrite the manuals and current-user docs so a fresh model can use the new interface efficiently without parsing raw storage or reverse-engineering workflows.
- Remove stale surface descriptions instead of preserving them as living text.

## Context
- The rendered help and quickstart are the primary manual for agents in this repo.
- The user explicitly does not want the repo to carry around a museum of removed commands and concepts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- internal/ergo/help.txt and internal/ergo/quickstart.txt match the new runtime interface and examples exactly.
- README, docs/spec.md, fixtures, and any other current-user docs/examples align with the new interface and forward vocabulary.
- Deleted surface is removed from current docs rather than described as a migration story, except where a short compatibility note is truly necessary.
- The docs teach a simple inspect-then-mutate workflow that keeps models on the CLI instead of raw .ergo parsing.

## Validation Gates
- go run ./cmd/ergo --help
- go run ./cmd/ergo quickstart
- rg -n "new epic|--body-stdin|tasks:\[|list --containers|old surface terms to be removed" README.md docs internal/ergo testdata skills

## Goal
- Add the regression coverage needed to make this hard cutover safe.
- Prove the new interface works for both happy paths and the easy-to-miss failure modes that models trigger.

## Context
- This change rewires command parsing, stdin semantics, markdown planning, and compatibility behavior.
- The highest risk is not one core algorithm; it is drift between command routing, docs, and storage behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Integration tests cover the new runtime examples from the manuals: new task with stdin body, claim, set with result, list/list --all, show, and plan --file.
- Parser and validation tests cover malformed inline JSON, missing required title, bad state/claim combinations, missing/invalid plan file content, duplicate chunk titles if disallowed, and removed-surface failures.
- Compatibility tests cover reading old logs, mutating them, and compact behavior across old/new event mixes.
- Fixture or smoke coverage catches stale script/doc examples that teach deleted syntax.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./cmd/ergo/...
- go test ./internal/ergo/...
- go test ./...

## Goal
- Finish the cutover cleanly once implementation and coverage land.
- Leave the repo teaching only the new interface, with a high-confidence test pass and minimal conceptual cruft.

## Context
- The user wants to move forward, not preserve a large in-repo explanation of deleted behavior.
- This task is where naming cleanup, release-facing notes, and final confidence checks happen.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Remove stale vocabulary, comments, fixtures, and docs that still describe the deleted interface unless they are required for compatibility tests.
- Run the full test suite and any repo-standard verification needed for confidence.
- Update release-facing docs/changelog/version text to describe the new surface without a long migration narrative.
- Leave a short completion note summarizing any intentionally retained compatibility seams.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./...
- git diff --check
- Manual: final pass on go run ./cmd/ergo --help and quickstart

## Goal
- Convert draft-of-new-help-text.md into a precise implementation contract before code work spreads.
- Resolve any ambiguity in the draft into explicit parser and output rules so downstream tasks can be mechanical.

## Context
- The draft introduces positional JSON args for new/set/plan, stdin-as-body semantics, and a narrower field vocabulary.

  - `set <id> [json]` updates one task; stdin, when piped, replaces the body.

  - `show <id>` and `list ...` keep their current read roles; `claim`, `sequence`, `init`, `where`, `prune`, and `compact` remain.

data.title: Make Ergo robust under parallel agent contention
Add bounded lock waiting and timeout controls
data.body:
## Goal
- Make normal parallel agent writes serialize inside Ergo instead of surfacing `lock busy`.
- Preserve an explicit fail-fast mode for hooks and scripts that need it.

## Context
- Current writes use non-blocking `flock` and return `ErrLockBusy` immediately.
- This task establishes the shared lock API and CLI option used by later work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `GlobalOptions` carries a lock timeout with a default of 30 seconds.
- A global `--lock-timeout <duration>` flag accepts Go-style durations such as `0`, `250ms`, `5s`, and `30s`.
- Mutating commands wait with bounded retry and small jitter until the timeout expires.
- `--lock-timeout 0` preserves fail-fast behavior and returns the existing lock-busy error path.
- Waiting holds no repo state open except the lock file descriptor.
- No environment variable is added for this behavior.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./...

data.title: Make each mutating CLI invocation one transaction
data.body:
## Goal
- Ensure a single command either applies its whole logical change or no change.
- Remove split-lock command paths that can leave partially applied work under contention.

## Context
- `new task` currently creates the task, then may reacquire the lock to apply extra JSON fields.
- Multi-event commands should validate against the latest replayed graph and append their full event batch from one lock scope.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `new task` applies title, body, epic, claim, state, and result updates under one exclusive lock when those fields are provided.
- `claim`, `set`, `sequence`, `plan`, `prune --yes`, and `compact` keep validation and writes inside a single lock scope.
- Command output is built from the same logical result that was written, not from an unlocked reread.
- Existing JSON output shapes remain compatible.
- Errors during validation or append leave the event log unchanged for that command.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./...

data.title: Make read commands use consistent snapshots
data.body:
## Goal
- Prevent `list` and `show` from observing a writer halfway through a multi-event update.
- Keep read-only commands cheap while making snapshots coherent under contention.

## Context
- Writers already serialize with an exclusive lock.
- Readers currently replay the log without taking a shared lock.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `list` and `show` acquire a shared lock while reading and replaying events.
- Read helpers avoid double-locking when called from code that already holds an exclusive lock.
- A reader waits behind an active writer using the same timeout policy, then reads a coherent snapshot.
- Read-only commands never write lock metadata or mutate the event log.
- Tests cover a held writer lock and verify read behavior is bounded, not hanging.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./...

data.title: Add lock holder diagnostics for timeout errors
data.body:
## Goal
- Make real contention explainable when a command times out waiting for the lock.
- Avoid unsafe stale-lock cleanup behavior.

## Context
- `flock` releases on process exit, so deleting `.ergo/lock` is not a safe recovery strategy.
- Diagnostics should help users find the active writer without creating a second source of truth.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Exclusive lock acquisition records best-effort holder metadata: pid, command, agent, started_at, and lock mode.
- Timeout errors include holder metadata when available and fall back cleanly when missing or malformed.
- Metadata is overwritten on each successful exclusive lock acquisition and cleared or marked released on unlock.
- Ergo never deletes `.ergo/lock` as a recovery path.
- JSON error behavior remains deterministic for existing machine consumers.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./...

data.title: Add contention-focused integration coverage
data.body:
## Goal
- Prove the new behavior with process-level tests that resemble parallel agent usage.
- Keep old fail-fast coverage for callers that opt into it.

## Context
- Existing concurrency coverage already checks that racing claims do not double-claim.
- The new contract should verify both waiting defaults and explicit fail-fast mode.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Racing `claim --agent` processes succeed or return `no_ready`; none fail with `lock busy` under the default timeout.
- Racing `new task`, `set`, and `list/show` commands do not corrupt the log or expose invalid snapshots.
- `--lock-timeout 0` still produces the documented lock-busy path when another process holds the lock.
- Timeout tests use bounded sleeps and do not make the suite flaky or slow.
- The event log remains valid JSONL after the race tests.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./...

data.title: Update manuals and maintainer docs for the new concurrency contract
data.body:
## Goal
- Make `ergo --help`, `ergo quickstart`, the spec, and architecture notes match the implementation.
- Teach agents that ordinary parallel use is safe without asking them to implement retry loops.

## Context
- The current docs say lock acquisition is fail-fast and callers should retry.
- After implementation, fail-fast is an opt-in mode rather than the default.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `internal/ergo/help.txt` documents `--lock-timeout` without making the help screen noisy.
- `internal/ergo/quickstart.txt` explains the default wait behavior, fail-fast option, and timeout diagnostics.
- `docs/spec.md` and `docs/architecture.md` describe bounded lock waiting, shared read snapshots, and the no-stale-lock-deletion rule.
- Tests that assert help or quickstart output are updated.
- No tombstone/history prose is added; docs describe current behavior only.

## Validation Gates
- go test ./...

## Scope
- Make Ergo robust when multiple agents or subagents issue commands against the same .ergo directory in parallel.
- Preserve the local append-only JSONL storage model and single-writer coordination.
- Keep ordinary users and agents from needing their own retry loops.

## Decisions
- Keep the existing single file-lock coordination model.
- Mutating commands wait up to 30 seconds by default before returning a timeout.
- `--lock-timeout 0` is the explicit fail-fast path for hooks and scripts.
- Each mutating CLI invocation should validate and append its full logical event batch under one exclusive lock.
- `list` and `show` should read under a shared lock so snapshots are coherent.
- Add best-effort lock holder diagnostics, but never delete `.ergo/lock` as recovery.

## Non-Goals
- No daemon, queue server, SQLite migration, or cross-machine locking protocol in this feature.
- No requirement that users stop launching multiple agents or subagents.
- No broad storage rewrite beyond the helpers needed for bounded waits and coherent snapshots.

## Risks
- Avoid nested lock helpers that deadlock when a writer calls read/replay logic.
- Keep contention tests bounded and deterministic.
- Preserve existing JSON output contracts for machine consumers.

## Goal
- Make normal parallel agent writes serialize inside Ergo instead of surfacing `lock busy`.
- Preserve an explicit fail-fast mode for hooks and scripts that need it.

## Context
- Current writes use non-blocking `flock` (`util.go:withLock`, LOCK_NB) and return `ErrLockBusy` immediately.
- This task establishes the shared lock API and CLI option used by later work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `GlobalOptions` carries a lock timeout with a default of 30 seconds.
- A global `--lock-timeout <duration>` flag is registered in `cmd/ergo/cmd_root.go` and accepts Go-style durations such as `0`, `250ms`, `5s`, and `30s`.
- Mutating commands wait with bounded retry and small jitter until the timeout expires.
- `--lock-timeout 0` preserves fail-fast behavior and returns the existing lock-busy error path.
- Waiting holds no repo state open except the lock file descriptor.
- No environment variable is added for this behavior.

## Notes / Accepted limitations
- `flock` retry-with-jitter gives no FIFO fairness; under sustained contention a waiter may starve until timeout. Acceptable at ergo's scale (a handful of agents). Do not add a fairness/queue mechanism.

## Validation Gates
- go test -race ./...

## Goal
- Prevent `list` and `show` from observing a writer halfway through a multi-event update.
- Keep read-only commands simple by reusing the one existing lock, not a second lock mode.

## Context
- Writers already serialize with an exclusive lock.
- Readers currently replay the log without any lock (`loadGraph` called directly).
- Design decision: use the SAME exclusive lock for reads as for writes. At ergo's scale a full replay is ~3-15ms, so serializing concurrent reads costs nothing meaningful and avoids a shared/exclusive (LOCK_SH) protocol, lock downgrade, and most nested-lock risk.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `list` and `show` acquire the exclusive lock while reading and replaying events, using the timeout policy from OWF4CP.
- Read/replay is split into a lockless core (safe to call while already holding the lock) and a top-level locked wrapper used by read commands; writers keep calling the lockless core inside their own lock scope. No path takes the lock twice.
- Read-only commands never write lock metadata or mutate the event log.
- A reader waits behind an active writer, then reads a coherent snapshot; it never hangs unbounded.
- Tests cover a held writer lock and verify read behavior is bounded, not hanging.

## Notes
- The 30s default now also bounds reads. This is acceptable; document in 7CJIPG that a read can wait behind a writer.

## Validation Gates
- go test -race ./...

## Goal
- Ensure a single command either applies its whole logical change or no change.
- Remove split-lock command paths that can leave partially applied work under contention.

## Context
- `new task` currently creates the task under one lock (`createTask`), then reacquires the lock via `applySetUpdates` to apply extra JSON fields (`commands_create.go:94-107`).
- `set` with a result path calls `writeResultEvent` (which takes its OWN lock, `storage.go:509`) and then a SECOND `withLock` for remaining fields (`commands_work.go:255-269`).
- Multi-event commands should validate against the latest replayed graph and append their full event batch from one lock scope.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `new task` applies title, body, epic, claim, state, and result updates under one exclusive lock when those fields are provided.
- `claim`, `set`, `sequence`, `plan`, `prune --yes`, and `compact` keep validation and writes inside a single lock scope.
- `writeResultEvent` and the `new task` extra-fields path are refactored into LOCKLESS cores called inside the caller's single lock scope (no helper takes the lock a second time; avoids the nested-lock deadlock the epic flags).
- Command output is built from the same logical result computed inside the lock, not from an unlocked reread (removes the post-write `loadGraph` at `commands_work.go:75,118` and `commands_create.go`).
- A batch of events is appended as a single buffered write so a concurrent reader cannot observe a partially applied batch.
- Existing JSON output shapes remain compatible.
- Errors during validation or append leave the event log unchanged for that command.

## Coordination
- This task and 7WDJ2E both edit the read/replay-and-lock helpers. Do N4LBCP first (it establishes the lockless-core split); 7WDJ2E then adds the read wrapper. Do not run them in parallel.

## Validation Gates
- go test -race ./...

## Goal
- Make real contention explainable when a command times out waiting for the lock.
- Avoid unsafe stale-lock cleanup behavior.

## Context
- `flock` releases on process exit, so deleting `.ergo/lock` is not a safe recovery strategy.
- Diagnostics should help users find the active writer without creating a second source of truth.

## Acceptance Criteria
- On successful exclusive lock acquisition, the holder writes best-effort metadata (pid, command, agent, started_at, lock mode) into the CONTENT of `.ergo/lock` itself. flock is advisory and independent of file bytes, so this keeps a single file and adds no extra lock/file to manage.
- Metadata writes are strictly best-effort: any write error is ignored and never fails the command.
- Timeout errors include holder metadata when available and fall back cleanly when the lock file is empty, missing, or malformed.
- Metadata is overwritten on each successful exclusive lock acquisition and cleared or marked released on unlock.
- Ergo never deletes `.ergo/lock` as a recovery path.
- Holder metadata is advisory only; it is never read back as task state.
- JSON error behavior remains deterministic for existing machine consumers.

## Validation Gates
- go test -race ./...

## Goal
- Prove the new behavior with process-level tests that resemble parallel agent usage.
- Keep old fail-fast coverage for callers that opt into it.

## Context
- Existing concurrency coverage already checks that racing claims do not double-claim (`integration_test.go`, `TestPrune_ConcurrentRuns`).
- The new contract should verify both waiting defaults and explicit fail-fast mode.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Racing `claim --agent` processes succeed or return `no_ready`; none fail with `lock busy` under the default timeout.

- `--lock-timeout 0` still produces the documented lock-busy path when another process holds the lock.
- Prefer a deterministic held-lock barrier over timing sleeps: a test explicitly holds the lock, asserts a waiter blocks, releases, and asserts the waiter then succeeds. Reserve bounded sleeps only where a barrier is impractical.
- Tests assert bounded completion, not lock fairness/ordering.
- The event log remains valid JSONL after the race tests.

## Validation Gates
- go test -race ./...

## Goal

- Teach agents that ordinary parallel use is safe without asking them to implement retry loops.

## Context

## Validation
